bibliographic.citation: Walter Hübner, 'The desert of religion. Mit dem Bilde des Richard Rolle of Hampole. Nach drei Handschriften zum erstenmal herausgegeben. I. Handschriftenbeschreibung und Text,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 126 (1911), 58-74, with two audited unpaginated manuscript-image plates.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen
bibliographic.title: The desert of religion. Mit dem Bilde des Richard Rolle of Hampole. Nach drei Handschriften zum erstenmal herausgegeben. I. Handschriftenbeschreibung und Text

entities.description: The first installment of Hübner's edition: manuscript descriptions and attribution history, a 940-line base text drawn from Add MS 37049 with Cotton and Stowe variants in footnotes, and an afterword on the illustrated Richard Rolle tradition. The audited source scan also contains two unpaginated monochrome manuscript plates and two blank plate backs that are not represented in the XML.
entities.label: Walter Hübner, The Desert of Religion, part I (1911)

physicalDescription.extent: printed pp. 58-74, two unpaginated manuscript-image plates, and their blank backs within Archiv volume 126
notes: In the cached PDF the article occupies PDF pp. 71-91; the Cotton plate is PDF p. 72 with its blank back at p. 73, and the Stowe plate is PDF p. 79 with its blank back at p. 78.
The article begins on printed p. 58 and is signed 'Walter Hübner' at the end of printed p. 74.

rights.basis: The installment was published in 1911, and U-M describes the source editions selected for this collection as public-domain editions and marks the resulting collection No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: 1911 article, edited text, apparatus, commentary, typography, and printed plates
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: Copyright status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

bibliographic.citation: K. Schreiner and Walter Hübner, 'The desert of religion. II. Handschriftenkritik. Reim und Sprache. Zur Quellenkunde,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 126 (1911), 360-364.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen
bibliographic.title: The desert of religion. II. Handschriftenkritik. Reim und Sprache. Zur Quellenkunde

entities.description: The separately paginated continuation in the same Archiv volume. Schreiner's signed section analyzes manuscript relationships, rhyme, and Northern language and argues that A has the fewest errors and should underlie the text; Hübner's signed section analyzes biblical, patristic, and other sources.
entities.label: K. Schreiner and Walter Hübner, The Desert of Religion, part II (1911)

physicalDescription.format: Journal article continuation and scholarly commentary
notes: In the cached PDF the continuation occupies PDF pp. 380-384.
Schreiner signs after the language discussion on printed p. 362; Hübner signs after the source study on p. 364.

rights.basis: The installment was published in 1911, and U-M describes the source editions selected for this collection as public-domain editions and marks the resulting collection No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: 1911 textual, linguistic, and source commentary and typography
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: Copyright status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

access.accessMethod: Register for a Reader Pass, request the exact shelfmark, and consult in the London Reading Room; restrictions and a letter of introduction apply.
Use the British Library Digitisation Services form and request a complete color photographic surrogate of the present object, not only ff. 46r-66v.
access.contact: licensing@bl.uk for digitisation-service reuse questions; permissions@bl.uk for Imaging Services permission after or alongside the order
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction photography; availability, handling, format, color, and price subject to quote
notes: Request covers, spine, boards, pastedowns, every flyleaf and blank, and ff. i + 1-96 recto and verso; identify ff. 46r-66v as the work portion.
The service says original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned and that existing surrogates may otherwise yield black-and-white images; specify color explicitly.
Delivery does not authorize repository redistribution or print publication. Obtain written permission for the proposed Git and print-on-demand uses.

entities.description: The illustrated Northern English Carthusian miscellany used as Hübner's base witness. The Desert of Religion occupies ff. 46r-66v and is accompanied by 22 miniatures and 20 full-page tree diagrams; Richard Rolle is identified at f. 52v.

holding.notes: The exact current British Library record describes the whole volume and its target folios.

entities.label: London, British Library, Add MS 37049

physicalDescription.dimensions: 270 x 200 mm; text space approximately 230-235 x 140-160 mm
physicalDescription.extent: ff. i + 96, plus four unfoliated paper flyleaves at the beginning and four at the end
notes: The current catalog gives a Yorkshire or Lincolnshire Carthusian monastery as a possible origin and describes the dialect as Northern.
Scott's costume dating places the manuscript after about 1460-1470; the catalog normalizes the range to 1460-1500.
The target work is ff. 46r-66v. Hübner's historical broad locator 'fol. 46-67' includes the immediately following f. 67 material, which the current catalog treats separately.

entities.description: A separately bound parchment component containing Vado mori at ff. 1v-2r and The Desert of Religion at ff. 3r-23v, including the Twelve Abuses at f. 8r and Richard Rolle verses at f. 8v. Hübner collated it as C and reproduced its Richard Rolle page.

entities.description: An illustrated manuscript containing The Abbey of the Holy Ghost and The Desert of Religion. The current catalog locates the target at ff. 10r-31v and records that the quires are no longer in their original order.

holding.notes: The current record supplies whole-object codicology, current target folios, legacy digital-content status, and onsite conditions.

entities.label: London, British Library, Stowe MS 39

physicalDescription.dimensions: 265 x 190 mm; written space 205 x 150 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 34 folios, one unfoliated paper flyleaf at the beginning, and two at the end
notes: The target occupies current ff. 10r-31v; Hübner used the historical locator fols. 11-32.

The catalog says the original order was The Desert of Religion at ff. 33, 10-32 followed by The Abbey of the Holy Ghost at ff. 4-9; this codicological statement does not enlarge the current cataloged target-text boundary.
Thomas Astle's 1768 notes incorrectly attribute the manuscript's Middle English texts to John Alcock.
The current catalog records possible production for a Benedictine nunnery but gives origin only as England.
physicalDescription.support: parchment, with paper only at ff. 1-2 and 34

entities.description: A later source study responding to Hübner's edition. Allen maps extensive line ranges to the Speculum vitae, identifies additional borrowings from The Prick of Conscience, and proposes sources for the Twelve Abuses and other passages. It is not upstream of Hübner or the CME encoding.
entities.label: Hope Emily Allen, The Desert of Religion: Addendum (1911)

bibliographic.citation: Anne Mouron, 'Praying with Images: Some Medieval Advice,' The Way 55, no. 1 (January 2016), 20-30.
bibliographic.title: Praying with Images: Some Medieval Advice

entities.description: A public modern scholarly article that describes the work as a 943-line fifteenth-century devotional poem surviving in three Northern English illustrated manuscripts, explains its 22 conceptual sections and twenty tree diagrams, identifies major source texts, and analyzes its monastic and possibly Carthusian reading context.
entities.label: Anne Mouron, Praying with Images (2016)

rights.basis: The modern article is publicly downloadable but the PDF carries British Library image credits and no open license was found in the file or download page.
rights.component: 2016 article text, translations, photographs, layout, and PDF

notes: Keep the cached PDF private and gitignored; link to the provider rather than redistributing the file.

next_steps: Recheck the exact Add LARK/IIIF endpoint and Stowe legacy object after British Library recovery updates.
Ask British Library Reference Services whether a complete current Cotton part II surrogate exists but is not linked in the interim catalog.
If no public restoration date is available, order complete color photographic surrogates using the object-wide scopes recorded here.
notes: No manuscript image file was cached because no complete public deliverable was retrievable.
openQuestions.question: When will complete public image deliverables for Add MS 37049 and Stowe MS 39 return, and does a complete digital surrogate exist for the separately bound Cotton part II component?

next_steps: Describe the repository's public and commercial enablement purpose in the order or follow-up message.

Ask licensing@bl.uk and permissions@bl.uk to distinguish private research use, public Git hosting, illustration in scholarship, derivative formatting research, and image reproduction in print-on-demand books.
Preserve the written response as item-specific rights evidence before publishing any delivered files.
openQuestions.question: What exact written permissions and fees would govern Git redistribution, public download, scholarly quotation, and commercial or nonprofit print-on-demand use of any newly supplied British Library photographs?

next_steps: Consult Mouron's completed DPhil edition or forthcoming EETS edition and compare its lineation against Hübner and all three witnesses.
Identify whether the three-line difference reflects supplied witness readings, framing verse, or a different treatment of visual captions.
openQuestions.question: Why does Hübner's encoded main text contain 940 line elements while Mouron's modern edition lineation describes a 943-line poem?

next_steps: Consult Mouron's DPhil/EETS edition and the full linguistic and codicological studies of all three witnesses.
Keep Northern dialect, individual manuscript production, possible Carthusian context, and work composition as separate claims.
Do not revive the Walter Hilton, John Alcock, or Richard Rolle attributions without new evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can composition be localized and dated more precisely, and can the probable monastic or Carthusian author-compiler context be demonstrated rather than inferred?

next_steps: Verify the plate against restored or ordered Stowe images and the current pencil foliation.
Request a repository-confirmed old-to-current foliation concordance rather than extrapolating the one-folio offset across the altered quire order.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current folio corresponding to Hübner's Stowe plate caption 'fol. 16b, neben v. 283 ff.'?

reviewNotes: Hübner's title is an editorial title inferred from line 19; the medieval witnesses are not treated as having a verified title rubric.
Both immediate installments belong to Archiv volume 126. Archiv volume 127 is modeled only as the carrier of Hope Emily Allen's later addendum.
The three manuscripts are distinct illustrated witnesses. Richard Rolle's portrait and quoted verses are iconographic and source-context evidence, not evidence that Rolle authored the whole poem.

The requested reproduction scope is the complete present physical object or separately bound component, with the work's folios identified inside it; no cropped work-only manuscript cache was substituted.

lineage.summary: CME00075 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from two separately paginated installments in Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 126 (1911): Walter Hübner's manuscript description, edited poem, and afterword on pp. 58-74, and K. Schreiner's textual and linguistic criticism followed by Hübner's source study on pp. 360-364. Hübner based his continuous 940-line text on British Library Add MS 37049, ff. 46r-66v, and collated Cotton MS Faustina B VI, part II, ff. 3r-23v, and Stowe MS 39, now cataloged at ff. 10r-31v. The XML retains Hübner's textual apparatus and the prose studies but omits the source volume's two audited manuscript-image plates and their blank backs. A complete public scan of Archiv 126 is cached and maps both installments and the two plate leaves. A complete public scan, OCR, metadata, and scan-data set for Archiv 127 is cached around Hope Emily Allen's pp. 388-390 addendum, which identifies extensive direct borrowing from the Speculum vitae and other sources; this later study is collateral evidence, not an ancestor of the XML. Anne Mouron's complete public 2016 article is also cached as modern scholarly context. The British Library reports full legacy digital coverage for Add MS 37049, but the exact viewer and inferred new IIIF endpoint were unavailable when checked; Stowe's legacy images are likewise unavailable, and no complete public Cotton surrogate was found. Exact catalog, onsite consultation, complete-object reproduction-order, and permissions routes are recorded for all three manuscripts.

catalogingSubject.label: The Desert of Religion
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English devotional poem conventionally called The Desert of Religion, as transmitted in three illustrated British Library manuscripts and represented by Walter Hübner's two-part 1911 edition and University of Michigan CME item CME00075. The record treats the poem as one intellectual work with a compilatory textual and visual design. It retains separately modeled German editorial framing, manuscript criticism, and source analysis because the XML includes them, but it does not confuse those modern prose sections with the medieval poem, any one codex with the work as a whole, or Richard Rolle, Walter Hilton, John Alcock, the Speculum vitae, and other sources with secure authorship of the complete work.

contentParts.label: Hübner's manuscript description
contentParts.summary: Four German prose paragraphs identifying and describing the three witnesses, with Frederick Madden's English note rejecting the Cotton Walter Hilton attribution.

contentParts.label: The Desert of Religion
notes: The XML's 19 verse groups do not encode Mouron's 22 conceptual sections one-for-one.
The visual program occupies facing and accompanying manuscript surfaces; these text-only XML metrics do not measure all inscriptions in the manuscript images.
contentParts.summary: Hübner's 940-line critical base text of the anonymous Middle English devotional poem, with A readings in the continuous stream and extensive C/S variants in footnotes.

contentParts.label: Hübner's afterword on the illustrated tradition
contentParts.summary: German prose discussing the three manuscripts' images, Richard Rolle identifications and captions, and the relationship between the poem and its illustrated saints and trees, with fourteen quoted Middle English verse lines and a shield diagram.

contentParts.label: Schreiner's manuscript criticism, rhyme, and language study
notes: Schreiner's Scotland suggestion is explicitly conditional and is not promoted into a confirmed composition place.
contentParts.summary: German prose arguing that no surviving witness directly copies another, that C and S share an exemplar, that A has the fewest errors and should be the base, and that the poem's language is Northern.

contentParts.label: Hübner's source study
notes: Allen's later addendum substantially expands and corrects the source analysis by demonstrating extensive direct borrowing from the Speculum vitae.
contentParts.summary: German prose identifying biblical and patristic analogues, Jacobus de Voragine, allegorical desert and tree traditions, and the poem's stated compilation from many books.

assertion.notes: Mouron's modern description calls the text fifteenth-century; the surviving witnesses establish an upper bound but do not pinpoint one act of composition or compilation.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century in modern specialist description; exact compilation date unresolved

assertion.notes: The overlapping catalog ranges establish the earliest surviving attestation window verified here but do not prove whether Stowe or Cotton is physically the earlier copy.

dateStatements.display: Earliest surviving attestation window in the first half of the fifteenth century: Stowe MS 39 is dated 1400-1449 and Cotton part II 1425-1449

assertion.notes: Hübner's subtitle says the poem is published for the first time; part I prints the text and part II supplies criticism and source study later in the same volume.

dateStatements.display: 1911, first published by Walter Hübner in two installments within Archiv volume 126

extent.description: The XML's five stable intellectual/editorial parts are modeled below. It contains 19 LG and 954 L elements: 940 main-poem lines plus 14 verse lines quoted in Hübner's afterword. Mouron's 2016 article describes a 943-line poem divided conceptually into 22 sections (prologue, twenty trees, epilogue); that modern edition-level lineation is not substituted for the reproducible XML count.

formStatements.description: The cataloged medieval work is a rhyming-couplet poem. The substantial German prose is retained editorial framing and scholarship rather than part of the medieval expression.
German manuscript description with an extended English quotation from Frederick Madden.
Northern Middle English rhyming couplets arranged around a prologue, twenty conceptual tree sections, and an epilogue.
German prose commentary incorporating fourteen separately encoded Middle English verse lines and a textualized shield-of-faith diagram.
German textual and linguistic analysis with verse readings and a manuscript stemma.
German source commentary with substantial Latin quotations.

assertion.notes: The poem reorganizes doctrinal lists and borrowed passages into one illustrated wilderness-and-tree design.

scope.description: Quoted witness readings, poem lines, and captions inside editorial prose

notes: The preferred title is an established modern editorial title, not a medieval rubric.

German editorial prose and Latin quotations are documented in the edition-facing content descriptions and lineage rather than exposed as work-level language facets; the medieval poem's language is Middle English.
The whole-work form remains verse even though the encoded edition includes substantial prose framing and criticism, because those are editorial layers rather than the medieval expression.
Richard Rolle appears as a depicted and quoted devotional authority and as the author of one source passage, not as secure author of the complete poem.
The three witnesses' dates, origins, foliation, and image rights remain lineage facts and are not flattened into one work-level date or region.

next_steps: Consult Mouron's DPhil or completed EETS edition and compare its lineation against Hübner and all three witnesses.
Keep XML L counts, Hübner's printed lineation, visual-caption verse, and modern edited lineation distinct.
openQuestions.question: Why does Hübner's CME-encoded main text contain 940 lines while Mouron's modern edition lineation describes a 943-line poem?

next_steps: Review Mouron's full edition and the linguistic and codicological literature for all three witnesses.
Separate work composition from each manuscript's copying place and institutional context.
Retain Walter Hilton, John Alcock, Richard Rolle, and 'follower of Rolle' only as disputed or contextual claims unless new authorship evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous author-compiler's date, exact origin, monastic audience, and possible Carthusian affiliation be demonstrated more precisely?

next_steps: Obtain or regain complete color facsimiles of all three manuscripts.
Compare every textual and visual component against Mouron's modern edition and record witness-specific layouts and captions separately.
Do not infer absent image text from the CME XML or Hübner's two selected plates.
openQuestions.question: How should a future corpus edition represent the poem's text, tree inscriptions, hermit captions, images, and witness-specific layouts without reducing the imagetext to Hübner's linear transcription?

assertion.notes: Schreiner argues from rhyme, morphology, and vocabulary for Northern language. His conditional suggestion of Scotland from one rhyme is not normalized as a place claim.

assertion.notes: The edition supplies A as its base and C/S readings throughout; the exact division of physical collation labor is not stated.

subjects.label: Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments
Visual devotion, saints, hermits, and tree diagrams

summaries.value: The Desert of Religion is an anonymous Northern Middle English devotional poem that turns religious life into a spiritual wilderness and forest. Its rhyming couplets organize catechetical and ascetic teaching—virtues and vices, humility and pride, commandments, sacraments, works of mercy, confession, contemplation, and the perils of temptation—around twenty allegorical trees. In all three surviving fifteenth-century manuscripts, tree diagrams and images of saints and hermits form an integral visual program that invites meditative reading. The author-compiler openly says the treatise was taken from many books; Hope Emily Allen and Anne Mouron identify the Speculum vitae as its chief source alongside The Prick of Conscience, Richard Rolle's Emendatio vitae, and other devotional materials. CME00075 represents Hübner's 1911 edited poem and apparatus together with his German manuscript description and afterword, K. Schreiner's German textual and linguistic criticism, and Hübner's German source study; those editorial prose sections are retained context, not medieval authorship.

The XML encodes Hübner's Archiv 126 pp. 58-74 and the same volume's pp. 360-364. It contains a four-paragraph manuscript preface, a 940-line main poem with extensive footnote apparatus, an afterword with fourteen quoted verse lines and a shield diagram, Schreiner's manuscript and language criticism with a stemma, and Hübner's source study. It omits two unpaginated manuscript-image plates and their blank backs; the complete cached volume preserves those surfaces.

The medieval poem has a prologue, twenty conceptual tree sections, and an epilogue in Mouron's description. The encoded editorial unit surrounds it with Hübner's descriptions of Add MS 37049, Cotton Faustina B VI part II, and Stowe 39; discussion of Richard Rolle portraits and captions; Schreiner's stemma and Northern-language analysis; and Hübner's biblical, patristic, and allegorical source discussion.

The preferred title is Hübner's supplied 1911 title. The anonymous poem is not assigned to Walter Hilton, John Alcock, or Richard Rolle. Hübner's 940-line base text and Mouron's 943-line modern lineation are preserved as edition-specific extents pending direct reconciliation.

notes: Mouron explicitly says Hübner gave the work this title. It is an established editorial title, not a verified medieval rubric.
titles.supplied_by: Walter Hübner in 1911 and retained by modern scholarship

notes: The mixed English-German source title highlights the Richard Rolle image but does not attribute the entire poem to Rolle.

work.summary: The Desert of Religion is an anonymous Northern Middle English devotional poem that turns religious life into a spiritual wilderness and forest. Its rhyming couplets organize catechetical and ascetic teaching—virtues and vices, humility and pride, commandments, sacraments, works of mercy, confession, contemplation, and the perils of temptation—around twenty allegorical trees. In all three surviving fifteenth-century manuscripts, tree diagrams and images of saints and hermits form an integral visual program that invites meditative reading. The author-compiler openly says the treatise was taken from many books; Hope Emily Allen and Anne Mouron identify the Speculum vitae as its chief source alongside The Prick of Conscience, Richard Rolle's Emendatio vitae, and other devotional materials. CME00075 represents Hübner's 1911 edited poem and apparatus together with his German manuscript description and afterword, K. Schreiner's German textual and linguistic criticism, and Hübner's German source study; those editorial prose sections are retained context, not medieval authorship.
